segments.s617-g177: This conditional clause begins with اگر 'if'. تاب گیرد is a poetic causative/ stative sense: تاب here means 'endurance/strength' and گیرد (present/past-subjunctive form of گرفتن) means 'takes/obtains' — together 'if (my) heart can take/endure'. دل من places the experiencer 'my heart' directly after the verb (poetic word order). ز داد is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of/from justice' (داد = 'justice'), modifying تاب گرفتن.
segments.s617-g178: ازین پس is the fixed temporal phrase 'از این پس' = 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این + پس). مرا is the first-person object/dative 'to me' (here split in the input as م + را); را marks the definite object/beneficiary. تخت شاهی is a nominal phrase 'royal throne / kingship'; مباد is an optative/negative jussive meaning 'may it not be'. Put together the clause expresses a negative wish: 'may the kingship/throne not be mine henceforth.'
segments.s617-g179: This is a nominal phrase: زن 'woman' is the head noun followed by a sequence of epithets/ adjectives (فرخ 'fortunate', پاک 'pure') and the compound attribute یزدان‌پرست 'God‑worshipping / pious'. In classical/poetic Persian adjectives commonly follow the noun, and here they qualify the woman in apposition.
segments.s617-g180: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'again' (دگر + باره). بر گاو is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on the cow'. مالید is the past tense of مالیدن 'to rub/press' and takes دست 'hand' as its object; the word order here (مالید دست) is a poetic inversion where the verb precedes its object, yielding 'rubbed (her) hand on the cow.'
segments.s617-g181: به نام = 'in the name (of)'; به here has the sense 'in'/'by' marking the formulaic phrase. خداوند is 'God' and زردشت is the speaker's name (Zarathushtra); گفت is the verb 'said' in past tense (here introducing what follows). The prepositional phrase precedes the subject-verb sequence as a conventional opening formula.
segments.s617-g182: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. بیرون گذاری is a verbal phrase where گذاری (from گذاشتن/گذار) 'to put/bring' is used with بیرون 'out' to mean 'bring out / put outside' (here a hortative/imperative sense within reported speech). نهان is the direct object 'the hidden', and از نهفت is a redundant/poetic adverbial 'from concealment' (two near‑synonyms used for emphasis). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but functionally: 'that you bring the hidden out from hiding.'
segments.s617-g183: ز پستان گاوش is an ablative/possessive phrase: ز 'from', پستان 'udder/breast' and گاوش 'his/its cow' (گاو + possessive suffix). ببارید is the past tense of باریدن 'to fall / to rain' used metaphorically for drops of milk, and شیر is the noun 'milk' as the subject (literally 'from the cow's udder milk fell').
segments.s617-g184: زن میزبان 'the hostess / the host's woman' is the speaker; گفت is the past tense 'said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and دستگیر here is predicative/vocative 'helper, succorer' (an epithet addressed to someone). Together this is direct speech introduced briefly: the hostess said, 'O helper!'
segments.s617-g41: به + شبگیر together mark a temporal phrase 'at nightfall'; همی/به as a temporal preposition is common in classical Persian. هرمزد is used here as a proper name (appositive/time-phrase placing the event in that month), followed by خرداد ماه specifying the month (خرداد = the month Khordad, ماه = 'month').
segments.s617-g42: ازان دشت = 'from that plain' (ازان = 'from that/of that'); سوی = 'toward(s)'; دهی here is intended as the destination 'a village' (but the provided lemma senses do not include the noun 'village'); رفت‌شاه is a fused construction meaning 'the king went' (رفت + شاه, putting the action with the subject 'the king').
segments.s617-g43: ببیند is 3rd person singular present/imperative-form with the prefix ب- typical of epic Persian, here meaning 'he sees' or 'he perceives that...'. که introduces the content clause; اندر جهان داد هست = 'that in the world justice exists' with هست as the copula/existential verb.
segments.s617-g44: بجوید is 3rd person singular present 'he seeks' (ب- prefix + جوید). دل مرد یزدان‌پرست is a genitival noun phrase 'the heart of the God‑worshipping man' (مرد = 'man/person', یزدان‌پرست = 'God‑worshipper/pious'). The verb refers back to the justice just mentioned.
segments.s617-g45: همی + راند is a continuous/iterative verbal construction ('he kept driving/riding'). شبدیز را uses را as the definite object marker indicating Shabdiz is the thing being ridden/driven. نرم‌نرم is an adverbial reduplication meaning 'softly/gently' and modifies the verb.
segments.s617-g46: برین‌گونه = 'in this manner/so'; تا = 'until'; روز برگشت = 'the return of the day' (i.e. daybreak/morning's return) with برگشت used as a noun; گرم qualifies برگشت/روز as 'warm'. The whole phrase gives the temporal limit of the riding.
segments.s617-g47: همی‌راند again indicates continuous action 'he rode on'. حیران و پیچان are predicative/adverbial adjectives describing the manner/state (bewildered, twisting/winding), and به راه places the action 'on the road' or 'along the way'.
segments.s617-g48: به خواب و به آب = 'for/to sleep and for/to water' (repeated به marks the two objects of longing). آرزومند شاه is a postpositive adjective-noun sequence 'the king (شاه) desirous/longing (آرزومند)'; together they mean 'the king longing for sleep and water.'
segments.s617-g161: This is a locative phrase + verb with the noun (the thing) placed before the verb in classical word order: 'به پستانها در' = 'into the teats/udders', then the noun 'شیرخشک' (what would be inside) and the verb 'شود' 'becomes/enters' at the end. Note that the overt negation appears in the next segment, so although this fragment has no negative particle itself, it is negated by the following 'نبودی'.
segments.s617-g162: 'نبودی' is the past negative 'was not' (negating the preceding clause as well). 'به نافه درون' = 'in/into the navel' (locative), 'نیز' = 'also/too', and 'مشک' = 'musk'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: the negative verb heads the phrase and the location and item follow.
segments.s617-g163: Compound subject 'زنا و ربا' ('adultery and usury') is followed by the adverb 'آشکارا' and the verb 'شود' 'becomes/comes to be'. 'آشکارا شود' functions as 'become evident/manifest'; the clause sets a condition or circumstance for what follows.
segments.s617-g164: Subject 'دل نرم' ('soft/tender heart') is followed by a simile introduced by 'چون' ('like/as') and the comparison 'سنگ خارا' ('flinty stone'); final 'شود' marks the change of state 'becomes/turns into'. Word order is subject + simile + verb, typical of poetic registers.
segments.s617-g165: Initial locative 'به دشت اندرون' ('to/in the inner/plain interior') sets place. Then the clause follows SOV order typical of Persian: 'گرگ' (subject) 'مردم' (object) 'خورد' (ate/devoured). The verb 'خورد' here is the simple past/present sense 'to eat' describing the action.
segments.s617-g166: Subject 'خردمند' ('the wise') is followed by the present/jussive-form 'بگریزد' ('flee/should flee' or 'flees' in a general sense) and the prepositional phrase 'از بی‌خرد' ('from the foolish'). The verb carries the sense of habitual/general action (the wise tend to flee from fools).
segments.s617-g167: The verb 'شود' ('becomes/is made') appears first for emphasis; the noun phrase 'خایه در زیر مرغان' ('testicles in/under the birds') gives the location/subject and 'تباه' is the predicate adjective 'ruined/corrupt'. Together they mean 'the testicles become ruined under the birds.' 'خایه' is an older/coarser term used here in a concrete physical sense.
segments.s617-g168: 'هرانگه' = 'whenever' introduces a temporal clause; 'که' links it to the subordinate clause. 'بیدادگر گشت شاه' is an instance of inverted poetic word order: predicate 'بیدادگر گشت' ('became tyrannical') precedes the subject 'شاه' ('the king'), with 'گشت' indicating a change of state 'became/turned into.'
segments.s617-g169: چراگاه این گاو کمتر نبود is a simple predicative clause in which چراگاه (pasture) is followed by its possessor این گاو (this cow) without an explicit ezāfe — a common poetic ordering. کمتر نبود is a negative comparative predicate: کمتر (less/inferior) functions as the adjective, and نبود is the past-tense copula/verb ‘was not’.
segments.s617-g170: هم آبشخورش نیز بتر نبود contains two emphatic 'also' markers (هم ... نیز) framing the clause; آبشخورش is the noun آبشخور (watering-place) with the possessive suffix -ش (its). بتر نبود is the negative comparative ‘was not worse’ (بتر = worse, نبود = was not). The double particles add emphasis rather than change meaning.
segments.s617-g171: به پستان چنین خشک شد شیراوی places the locative phrase به پستان ('in/on the udder') and the adverb چنین ('thus') before the verb خشک شد ('dried up / became dry'), with شیراوی ('the milky one' = milky/milk-bearing) postponed to the end as the subject — a poetic inversion. شد is the past/pseudo-past sense 'became'.
segments.s617-g172: دگرگونه شد رنگ و آژیر اوی uses دگرگونه شد ('became different') as the verb phrase governing the coordinated nouns رنگ و آژیر ('color and cry/signal'). اوی is an archaic/pronoun-form functioning possessively ('his' / 'its') attached after the coordinated nouns. Word order (verb before possessed nouns) is typical of classical poetic syntax.
segments.s617-g173: چو ... شنود is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', followed by the subject بهرامشاه (King Bahram) and the object این سخنها ('these words'). شنود is an older/classical 3rd-person form of 'to hear' used here with past sense ('heard'); classical verbs often appear in this form in epic verse.
segments.s617-g174: پشیمانی آمدش ز اندیشه زود has the noun پشیمانی ('regret') as subject, آمدش = آمد + -ش ('came to him') where the enclitic -ش marks the indirect object 'to him'. ز اندیشه literally 'from thought' (i.e. into his mind), and زود modifies the verb meaning 'quickly/soon'.
segments.s617-g175: به یزدان چنین گفت کای کردگار is built with the dative phrase به یزدان ('to God') followed by چنین گفت ('thus said'); کای is a vocative 'O' addressing کردگار ('Creator'). Note that گفت here functions as 'said' (past), but the provided lemma senses do not list 'said' explicitly (see senses field).
segments.s617-g176: توانا و داننده روزگار is a pair of epithets modifying the vocative 'Creator': توانا ('powerful') and داننده ('knower', an active participle) linked by و ('and'), with روزگار ('time/fate') as the noun governed by داننده ('knower of fate/time'). Together they form the address 'O Creator, powerful and knower of fate.'
segments.s617-g145: Literal word order places the verb before subject and modifiers: بیاورد (past narrative 'brought') + گاو ('cow'). از چراگاه خویش = 'from its pasture': از is the poetic/genitive 'from', چراگاه 'pasture', and the possessive is split into خوی + ش, where ش is the 3rd‑person possessive suffix 'his/her/its'.
segments.s617-g146: فراوان گيا برد is compact: فراوان ('abundant') qualifies گیا ('grass/vegetation'); برد here functions as a past verb 'took/removed' in classical usage (listed senses are limited in the provided table). Then و بنهاد پیش = 'and (he) placed (it) before (her)': بنهاد is an archaic past tense of نهادن 'to place', and پیش = 'before/in front of'.
segments.s617-g147: به پستانش بر دست مالید is an action clause: به + پستانش marks the target 'to/onto its udder' (پستان + ش = 'its udder'), بر دست indicates the instrument/location 'on (his) hand', and مالید is the simple past 'he rubbed'. The clause ends with و گفت 'and said' linking this action to his speech.
segments.s617-g148: به نام = the fixed phrase 'in the name (of)'; here به is functioning as the relational particle in that idiom. خداوند بی‌یار و جفت is a reverential phrase: خداوند 'God', بی‌یار literally 'without a peer/companion', و جفت 'and without equal/match' — a short, formulaic epithet.
segments.s617-g149: تهی بود = 'was empty' (تهی 'empty' + بود past 'was'); پستان گاوش = 'the udder of his cow' (گا و + ش possessive suffix merged as گاوش). ز شیر uses poetic ز for 'of/from' to indicate lack of 'milk' (شیر). The clause says the udder lacked milk.
segments.s617-g150: دل میزبان جوان گشت پیر is a nominal phrase with a change-of-state verb: دل 'heart' of میزبان جوان 'the young host' is the topic; گشت (گشت) is the past-tense of شدن 'became/turned', and پیر 'old' is the resulting adjective. Word order places the possessed noun before the verb 'became'.
segments.s617-g151: چنین (an adverb) precedes the verb and means “thus/so.” گفت is a past‑tense verb (3rd sing.) “he said.” با is the older/prepositional marker here introducing the indirect object (“to/at”), and شوی is an archaic/inflected form of the verb شدن used optatively/subjunctively (literally “be/ become”); in this clause the sequence functions to set up reported speech addressed to someone. کای is the vocative particle “O,” immediately followed by کدخدای “village‑headman,” so the whole chunk frames the utterance as “Thus he said to him: ‘O village‑headman…’.”
segments.s617-g152: دل شاه گیتی is a stacked nominal phrase: دل (heart) + شاه گیتی (the king of the world; شاه modifies گیتی) — read as “the heart of the world‑king.” دگر شد is a two‑word predicate: دگر “other/different” plus شد (past of شدن) “became,” so “became different/was changed.” بران (an adverbial) means “thereupon/by that” and links the change to what was just mentioned, i.e. “thereby.”
segments.s617-g1: همی بود یک چند با مهتران: همی + بود is a common poetic past-frame meaning 'there was / for a while there was'. همی here gives a durative or habitual flavour ('for a little while' / 'there was'), بود is past tense 'was'. یک چند ('yek chand') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'a short while' or 'some time' and follows the verb complex. با introduces the accompanying group (مهتران) and comes after the temporal phrase — word order reflects older/poetic Persian (temporal phrase + verb + accompaniment).
segments.s617-g2: می‌روشن و جام و رامشگران: می‌روشن is a present/stative-form used here in narrative past sense (poetic use) meaning 'they shine' / 'they were bright' — in classical poetry present forms can describe ongoing appearance in the scene. و is the coordinating 'and'. جام and رامشگران are coordinated nouns; جام is the object/thing (cup/wine) associated with the scene and رامشگران ('those who soothe' / entertainers/comforters) names companions. The verbs governing this phrase are distributed to these nouns by coordination.
segments.s617-g3: بهار آمد و شد جهان چون بهشت: two short clauses joined by و. آمد is past 'came' with بهار as its subject. شد introduces the result clause 'جهان شد' = 'the world became', followed by a simile introduced by چون 'like' and the noun بهشت 'Paradise'. The sequence 'آمد و شد' is paratactic: spring's coming is immediately followed by the change in the world.
segments.s617-g4: به خاک سیه بر فلک لاله کشت: word order is somewhat inverted (typical of poetry). کشت is the past of 'to sow/plant' (here 'sowed/caused to grow'). لاله is the direct object (tulips). به خاک سیه is a locative/goal phrase 'on/into the dark soil' (به used as locative/into), and بر فلک is another locative/locution 'toward/up to the sky' (showing extent or poetic elevation). The agent is implicit (carried from the previous clause: 'spring' is understood as the doer).
segments.s617-g5: همه بومها پر ز نخجیر گشت: همه بومها is the subject phrase 'all the lands/regions'. پر ز is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر + ز meaning 'full of/from'), with ز marking the source/contents. نخجیر is the noun 'game' (animals sought by hunters) and گشت is the past 'became' — so grammatically: 'all the lands became full of game.'
segments.s617-g6: بجوی آبها چون می‌و شیر گشت: this phrase is compressed/poetic. بِجوی here functions as a locative/relational prepositional element ('in/along the streams') rather than the literal verb 'to seek' (so the surface lemma may mislead). آبها is the noun 'waters/streams'. چون introduces a comparison 'like', and the sequence می‌و شیر should be read as 'می و شیر' = 'wine and milk' (poetically joined); گشت is past 'became'. Altogether: 'in the streams the waters became like wine and milk.'
segments.s617-g7: گرازیدن گور و آهو به شخ: گرازیدن is a verbal noun/verb meaning to rush or charge (literally 'to act like a boar' — here 'to rush/bolt'). The coordinated nouns گور و آهو are the animals involved; in this context گور is the wild ass/onager (not 'grave'), and آهو is 'gazelle'. به شخ is an adverbial/onomatopoeic phrase indicating the manner or sound ('with a shakh' — a poetic sound or movement-mark). The verb sense is iterative/performative describing the animals' action.
segments.s617-g8: کشیدند بر سبزه هر جای نخ: کشیدند is past 'they drew/drew along' and here likely means 'drew (a line)/dragged' (poetic: leaving traces). بر سبزه is the locative 'over/on the greenery', and هر جای ('every place') modifies the following noun نخ ('thread/line'); نخ functions metaphorically for the streaks/traces the animals left. Word order is object-late as typical: verb then locative and quantified noun phrase.
segments.s617-g129: پراندیشه شد is a compound predicate: پراندیشه (literally “full of thought”) is an adjectival/state word and شد is the past tense of شدن ‘to become’; together they mean “became troubled/meditative.” زان (poetic contraction of از آن) is a prepositional/relational particle “because of / from that,” and سخن شهریار is a genitival noun phrase “the king’s speech” placed after the preposition.
segments.s617-g130: که introduces a clause of explanation/cause (“for/that”), followed by بد شد ‘became bad’ (شد = became). ورا is an enclitic object/pronominal adverb meaning “to/for him,” and نام here means “reputation/name.” زان again = “because of that,” and مایه‌کار is the agent causing the ill repute (a money‑person/financial backer). The clause reads: “that, because of that financier, his name became bad.”
segments.s617-g131: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening “thus said”; گفت is the third‑person past verb ‘said’ (here used in narrative). پس “then/thereupon” follows, and شاه یزدان‌شناس is the subject phrase ‘the God‑knowing (pious) king’ that performs the saying. Word order is typical poetic inversion: verb (گفت) precedes the subject.
segments.s617-g132: که introduces the reported content: از here means “from/of” (i.e. ‘by’ or ‘from the just one’ in this idiom). دادگر is an adjective/noun ‘the just (man)’; کس is an indefinite ‘anyone/anybody’. ندارد سپاس is a negated possession construction: ندارد ‘has not’ + سپاس ‘thanks/praise’ → ‘no one gives thanks/praise (to) the just.’ The object (سپاس) follows the verb‑negation as in classical Persian.
segments.s617-g133: درشتی کنم is first‑person present/future sense “I will act harshly / I will show roughness” with کنم (subjunctive/performative ‘I do’). زین here functions as ‘of/from this’ (classical contraction of از این); سخن is ‘matter/speech.’ ماه چند is an interrogative temporal phrase ≈ “for how long” (ماه = month/time period, چند = how many/how long). The whole segment asks “for how long shall I be harsh about this matter?”
segments.s617-g134: که introduces purpose/result: پیدا شود is a subjunctive/passive construction “may appear / may become manifest” (پیدا = apparent/found, شود = become). داد و مهر is a binomial “justice and kindness/favour” (داد = justice; مهر = kindness/affection). از گزند is a prepositional phrase “from harm,” so the clause means “so that justice and kindness may be seen/protected from harm.”
segments.s617-g135: شب تیره is a nominative phrase ‘a dark night’; ز introduces the cause/reason (“from/because of”), اندیشه پیچان is a compound noun phrase ‘twisting/entangling thoughts’ (پیچان modifying اندیشه). بخفت is the past tense (poetic) of خوابیدن ‘to sleep,’ here meaning “slept.” The line therefore says he passed a dark night because his thoughts were tangled.
segments.s617-g136: همه شب ‘all night/throughout the night’ sets the time frame. دلش ‘his heart’ (دل + enclitic ـش) is the subject; با ستم بود جفت is an idiomatic/past construction meaning his heart was paired/yoked with oppression—i.e. it lay with or was full of oppression. بود is the past copula. The adjective/nominal جفت here conveys accompaniment (‘paired with’).
segments.s617-g17: This is a serial prepositional list introduced by the poetic preposition ز (z, 'from'); the preposition is repeated for emphasis before each item (ز گور و ز غرم و ز آهو جهان). جهان here attaches to آهو (the phrase 'آهو جهان' = 'the world's gazelle'). Word order is poetic: repeated ز + noun, rather than a single preposition governing a coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s617-g18: بپرداختند is a past tense verb with a plural ending (-ند); classical epic Persian often uses plural or 'majestic' forms where modern prose would use singular, and the grammatical subject follows the verb (postposed subject). آن دلاور مهان is the postverbal subject phrase ('that brave one / that great hero'); مهان is an epithet in apposition. The construction thus places the action first (sprang/acted) and then names the actor.
segments.s617-g19: This clause begins with a numeral expression + temporal conjunction: سه دیگر ('three more/three others') followed by چو ('when'); بفروخت is past-tense (3rd person) and takes خورشید تاج (literally 'the sun (as) crown') as its object — the word order is verb after object, typical of classical Persian poetry where topical elements can precede the verb.
segments.s617-g20: زمین زرد شد is a straightforward past copular clause: subject (زمین) + adjective (زرد) + شد (became). The following coordinate phrase کوه و دریا چو عاج is a simile: 'mountain and sea (were) like ivory' — چو here functions as 'like/as' to introduce the comparison.
segments.s617-g21: به نخچیر شد is a light verb construction meaning 'went to the hunt / set out for hunting': به + noun نَخچیر ('game; hunting') followed by شد ('became/went'), so the whole means 'went hunting' or 'set out to take game'. شهریار دلیر is the following nominal phrase (the subject) describing who did this.
segments.s617-g22: این جمله has a simple past transitive clause: یکی ('a certain' / 'one') introduces the object, اژدها (dragon) is the direct object, and دید is the past verb 'saw'. The final phrase چون نره شیر is a simile introduced by چون = 'like', with نره شیر = 'male lion' describing the dragon's appearance.
segments.s617-g23: This is a locative description: به بالای او ('on his upper part / above him') gives the location, followed by موی ('hair') as the thing located. زیر سرش ('under his head') is another locative modifier attached by the enclitic -ش ('his'); the sequence is compressed poetically (location phrases and possessed nouns appear in flexible order).
segments.s617-g24: دو پستان is the noun phrase 'two breasts'. بسان ('like/as') introduces the simile, زنان ('women') supplies the comparison, and از برش is a compound locative 'from/of his side' (از + بر + -ش), indicating these breasts were on his flank. Word order places the simile phrase immediately after the noun it modifies, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s617-g97: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شد + کشته is an inverted poetic passive construction equivalent to کشته شد ('was killed'), with شد functioning as the auxiliary verb. دیگی هریسه follows: دیگی (cauldron) + هریسه (harissa, a porridge) and بپخت is a third-person narrative/optative form of پختن used in epic poetry to indicate that the cooking occurred ('they set to cook' / 'it was cooked').
segments.s617-g98: برند is the 3rd-person plural classical/narrative form of بردن meaning 'they took' or 'they fetched'; it governs the object آتش 'fire'. از introduces the origin/ source 'from', followed by هیزم 'firewood' qualified by نیم‌سخت 'somewhat hard' (compound adjective after the noun is usual in poetry). The clause means 'they fetched fire from somewhat hard firewood.'
segments.s617-g99: بیاورد is a 3rd-person narrative/past form meaning 'she/they brought' (classical narrative style). چیزی is the indefinite object 'something'. بر + شهریار marks direction/recipient 'to the king' (بر here = 'toward/to'). Word order verb–object–prepositional phrase is typical in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s617-g100: برو is the imperative 'go' (or an emphatic narrative 'go and ...'); what follows is a coordinated noun phrase listing what is to be fetched: خایه (testicle) و تره (and herbs). جویبار can be read as a modifier or appositive to تره ('stream-herbs' / 'herbs of the stream') or as a linked noun in the list; the sequence is a terse imperative instruction typical of verse.
segments.s617-g101: یکی پاره ('one piece' or 'a single slice') is the noun phrase; بریان is a past-participle/adjective 'roasted'. ببرد is the 3rd-person classical/narrative form of بردن ('he/they took/carried away'). از بره is the source phrase 'from the lamb'. The clause reads 'they took one roasted piece from the lamb.'
segments.s617-g102: همان پخته ('that very cooked') + چیزی ('thing/something') forms a determiner + participle + noun phrase; که introduces a relative clause modifying چیزی. بد یکسره is an adverbial/qualifying phrase: بد here functions as 'poorly/ill' modifying how it was cooked, and یکسره 'completely/altogether' intensifies it. Altogether: 'that cooked thing which was altogether badly (done).'
segments.s617-g103: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بهرام is the subject. دست از خورشها بشست is a fixed construction: دست بشستن از چیزی can mean literally 'to wash one's hands from (the) stews' or idiomatically 'to cease from the stews/meal'; بشست is the simple past 3rd-person 'he washed'. Word order places the object phrase (دست از خورشها) before the verb in a poetic inversion.
segments.s617-g104: همی بود is a habitual/continuous past 'he was / remained' (همی adds continuous or habitual nuance). بی‌خواب 'sleepless' and ناتن‌درست 'unsound/ill' are predicate adjectives linked by و 'and', so the clause states his continuing condition: 'he remained sleepless and unwell.'
segments.s617-g9: همه (determiner) precedes the plural noun جویباران to mean 'all the streams.' پر از is the predicate phrase 'full of' (پر used predicatively with از introducing the content). مشک دم is a genitive/compound: 'breath of musk' = 'musk-scented breath.' The clause is verbless (poetic ellipsis of بود/شد).
segments.s617-g10: بسان is a simile preposition 'like/as' introducing گل نارون 'the flower of the elm' (noun + genitive-like epithet). می‌به خم is a poetic compound: می‌به refers to a wine-/quince-related poetical image and خم here denotes the jar/neck (the curve of the vessel); together they form the image 'toward the curve/neck of the wine-jar.' The whole segment is a simile modifying the previous clause.
segments.s617-g11: بگفتند is the simple past 'they said.' با here introduces the addressee/participant and is used in the sense 'to/with' (older poetic usage). شاه بهرام گور is the addressee: شاه 'king' + proper name بهرام with the epithet گور (here 'Gūr' = 'onager' as an epithet).
segments.s617-g12: که introduces the reported clause 'that…'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' (copulative here). دیر هنگام is the adverbial phrase 'late (in the) hour' = 'late in the day.' نخچیر گور is a compound 'nakhchīr of Gūr' meaning 'the onager's hunt/foray' (نخچیر = hunt/prey, گور = onager), completing the reported content: 'that the onager's hunt became late.'
segments.s617-g13: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the content of the answer. مردی هزار is a fronted noun + numeral ('a man, a thousand')—the phrase will be linked to the verb in the next segment; poetic word order places the quantity after the noun.
segments.s617-g14: گزین کرد باید is a poetic inversion of the more regular باید گزین کرد or باید گزینید: باید 'it is necessary' + گزین کرد 'to choose/select' (گزین + light verb کرد). The order here emphasizes the necessity. ز لشکر سوار is a prepositional phrase 'from the host/army (ز لشکر) [the] horsemen (سوار)'—سوار is used as the noun 'horsemen/ riders.' The whole means 'one must choose a thousand horsemen from the army.'
segments.s617-g15: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward.' تور here means 'net' (a hunting net) and is the object of سوی. شد is the past of شدن 'became/turned/moved' used to indicate the king's movement toward that direction. شاه نخچیرجوی is the subject with an epithet: شاه 'the king' followed by نخچیرجوی 'hunt-seeking' or 'hunter' (epithet), yielding 'the king, hunter of the chase, turned toward the net.' Word order is poetic (verb between prepositional phrase and subject).
segments.s617-g16: جهان گشت is 'the world became' (گشت = became/turned). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once.' پر از گفت‌وگوی repeats the 'full of' construction (پر از) with گفت‌وگوی 'conversation/talk' as the content: 'the world became entirely filled with conversation.'
segments.s617-g73: This segment contains two verbs in sequence: بیامد (he came) + نشست (he sat). Classical Persian often places consecutive perfective verbs like this to narrate successive actions. The prepositional cluster از بر + آن حصیر literally gives the place 'upon that mat' (از بر functioning here in a classical/poetic sense marking location, with بر = 'on/upon' and آن = 'that').
segments.s617-g74: This string locates the sitting with respect to the house and an old man: بدر here is an archaic/poetic spelling compacting به در ('to/at the door'), hence 'at the house-door'; بر پای means 'at the foot (of)'; بد modifies مرد as 'ailing/sick' and پیر is the noun's age attribute 'old'. Word order is typical classical Persian: local phrase (بدر خانه) then a phrase giving the relationship (بر پای) and the described person (بد مرد پیر).
segments.s617-g75: بیاورد is a past narrative verb 'he brought' and takes خوانی (here a nominal 'a meal/banquet/spread') as its object; بنهاد راست is another past verb phrase 'he set (it) down straight/properly' with راست qualifying the manner ('properly/straight'). The pair of verbs narrates his deliberate action of presenting and arranging food.
segments.s617-g76: This is an imperative/report of an order. برو functions as a vocative/imperative 'go/fetch/bring' addressed to an attendant, followed by a simple list of items: تره (greens), سرکه (vinegar), نان (bread) and ماست (yogurt). In the manuscript the yogurt appears split into ما + ست; here they together form the noun 'ماست'.
segments.s617-g77: بخورد is a past-tense narrative verb 'he ate' (classical form used in narrative), followed by اندکی نان 'a little bread' as its object. The conjunction و links to نالان ('lamenting' / 'moaning') and then بخفت 'he slept/fell asleep' — the participial adjective nâlân modifies his state as he falls asleep.
segments.s617-g78: به + دستار marks instrument/location 'with/by his turban'; چینی here describes the turban (probably 'folded' or 'in folds' in context, an older/poetic adjective) — the sense provided in the wordlist doesn't match exactly, so it is treated as a context-specific cloth/folding descriptor. رخ اندر نهفت is a verbal phrase 'he hid/concealed (اندر نهفت) his face (رخ)' — the verb نهفت is past narrative 'hid'.
segments.s617-g79: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; از خواب بیدار شد is a complete past event 'he awoke from sleep' (بیدار = 'awake', شد = past auxiliary 'became/was'). زن is the subject 'the woman' (here likely 'his wife'), and بشوی is an imperative 'wash (yourself)' directed to the sleeper; the imperative verb form is compressed into classical diction.
segments.s617-g80: همی is a frequent narrative particle (here giving immediacy/continuity) followed by گفت 'she said' (in context the verb is used narratively). کای is the vocative 'O', and the phrase زشت ناشسته روی addresses the addressee: زشت 'ugly', ناشسته 'unwashed' modifying روی 'face'. The whole clause reports the woman's exclamation rebuking the sleeper's unwashed face.
segments.s617-g137: بدانگه که is a temporal connector: بدانگه (thereupon/then) + که (that/when) introduces the subordinate clause. شب چادر مشک‌بوی is a compact, poetic noun phrase: شب (night) governs a following noun phrase چادر مشک‌بوی (the musk‑scented veil/cover), so the order expresses 'the musk‑scented veil of the night' rather than a literal sequence 'night veil musk‑scented'.
segments.s617-g138: بدرید is a past action ('was torn' / 'tore'); in this context it has a passive/middle sense — the veil is rent. و simply links clauses. بر چرخ بنمود روی is an inverted, idiomatic phrasing: بر چرخ (on/upon the heaven/sky) + بنمود (3sg past 'revealed/appeared') + روی (face) literally 'showed face on the sky' — subject is understood from the previous noun phrase (the veil/night).
segments.s617-g139: بیامد is simple past 'came'. زن 'woman' (here: the wife) is the subject. از خانه 'from the house' gives origin. با شوی is a phrase meaning 'to/with her husband' in the narrative (classical Persian often uses با + person to mark accompaniment or address); note that the supplied lemma for شوی looks like a verbal root, but here it functions as a nominal 'شو[هر]' ('husband') in context. گفت is the past-tense reporting verb 'said'.
segments.s617-g140: که introduces the reported content ('that ...'). هرکاره و آتش آر is a coordinated nominal phrase: هرکاره ('one who does anything' / 'every kind of work' or as an epithet) joined by و to آتش آر ('fire‑desire' — literally 'fire‑longing', here likely 'the (household) fire or passion/ardour'). از نهفت is a past participial/adjectival phrase meaning 'was hidden / concealed'. The whole clause reports that these things were hidden.
segments.s617-g141: ز هرگونه تخم is a genitive/partitive expression: ز (from/of) + هرگونه (every kind) + تخم (seed). اندرافگن is an obscure/archaic verb form here meaning something like 'placed/put/cast' (classical diction); به آب 'into the water' uses به in the directional sense 'into/towards'. The phrase as a whole marks seeds of every kind being put into water.
segments.s617-g142: نباید is an impersonal negative 'one must not / it must not'. که introduces the subordinate clause with the verb بیند (subjunctive/present of 'to see' used after نباید). ورا is the clitic/old object form 'it / to it' placed before the subject noun (classical word order), and آفتاب (sun) is the logical subject: literally 'the sun must not see it.'
segments.s617-g143: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. تا plus a following verb in the subjunctive (بدوشم) expresses 'until I ...' — here بدوشم is the first‑person form connected to دوش (milking) and means 'I milk / I have milked'. ازین (از + این) means 'from this' (ازین گاو = 'from this cow'). شیر here is 'milk' (not 'lion').
segments.s617-g144: تو is the vocative/2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. این کار 'this task' is the object being referred to. هرکاره is used as a vocative epithet ('O jack‑of‑all‑trades' / 'you who do everything'). آسان مگیر is a negative imperative: مگیر (do not take) with آسان (lightly/easily) — 'do not treat [this] lightly.'
segments.s617-g209: هرانکس که introduces a free relative clause meaning 'whoever' (هرانکس) + relative particle که; این تازیانه is the direct object ('this/that whip'); بدید is the past tense of 'to see' (archaic/political poetic form of دیدن) placed at the clause end, giving a simple past relative clause 'whoever saw this whip.' Word order is typical Classical Persian SOV within the relative clause.
segments.s617-g210: به marks the beneficiary/indirect object ('to/for'); بهرامشاه is the addressee/recipient 'King Bahram'; آفرین is a noun 'acclamation, praise' and گسترید is the past of گستر(دن) 'to spread/extend' used idiomatically with آفرین (آفرین گستردن) = 'to applaud/praise'. The verb is past, third-person, agreeing with the generic subject from the previous clause.
segments.s617-g211: پیاده functions adverbially 'on foot'; همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; پیش شیب دراز is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in front of/before the long slope' with adjective دراز following the noun شیب ('long slope'). The clause order places the adverbial first, then subject, then the locative phrase.
segments.s617-g212: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/advanced'; و links the coordinated verb phrase; بردند here is used idiomatically with نماز to mean 'they performed/undertook (the prayer)' though its base sense is 'to take/carry'; یک‌یک 'one by one' qualifies the following noun; نماز is the object 'prayer'. Verbs appear before the object in this poetic register.
segments.s617-g213: زن و شوی is a coordinated subject 'the woman and the husband' (شوی here denotes the spouse; the listed lemma senses do not include 'husband' so sense must be flagged accordingly). گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing reported speech; این is the demonstrative 'this'; به جز شاه نیست is a predicative negative clause 'is nothing except the king' (جز ... نیست = 'is none other than'). Note that poetic usage may allow forms and agreements that differ from modern colloquial patterns.
segments.s617-g214: چنین چهره = 'such a face/countenance' (چنین = 'such', چهره = 'appearance'); جز introduces the exclusive 'except/only'; درخور is an adjective meaning 'appropriate/suitable' modifying گاه ('time/occasion'); نیست is the copular negative 'is not', giving the sense 'such a countenance is appropriate only for a fitting/royal occasion.' Word order places the adjective after the noun (درخور گاه) as is common.
segments.s617-g215: پر از شرم is a prepositional phrase 'full of shame' modifying the subject; رفتند is the past 'they went/left'; هر دو explicitly marks the subject 'both'; ز راه is poetic prepositional use of ز (= از) 'from the road'. The sequence shows the descriptive phrase fronted, followed by the verbal action and the specification of who ('both') and whence ('from the road').
segments.s617-g216: پیاده marks the manner 'on foot'; دوان is an adverb/participle 'running/rapidly' (here indicating they ran); تا introduces a limit 'until/as far as'; به نزدیک شاه = 'to the vicinity/near the king' with به + نزدیک + noun. The clause thus means they ran on foot up to the point of being near the king.
segments.s617-g25: کمان را: the direct object كمان 'bow' is marked by the object marker را (definite object). به زه کردن is a light-verb construction meaning 'to bring the bow to/the bowstring' = 'to draw the bow (string)'; کرد is the past tense main verb 'did/made' completing that action. و connects this clause to the following action; تیر خَدنْگ is a compound noun phrase 'tire- khadang' = 'a dagger-like (heavy) arrow' serving as the object of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s617-g26: بزد is the simple past 'he struck/shot' used for firing the arrow. The doubled بر is a poetic placement: the first بر functions as a verbal/adverbial 'upon/at' attached to زد, the second begins the prepositional phrase بر اژدها 'at/on the dragon' (the duplication serves meter/emphasis). بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately/without delay' modifying the whole action.
segments.s617-g27: دگر here means 'again' (an adverb). تیز modifies the verb as 'sharply/quickly'. زد is the past 'he struck'. بر میان سرش is the prepositional phrase 'upon/into the middle of its head' with میان 'middle' and the pronominal clitic ش 'its' attached to سر.
segments.s617-g28: فروریخت is an intransitive past 'it collapsed/fell down'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as' and pairs with آب خون 'water, blood' — poetically 'blood like water' meaning blood poured forth. از + برش 'from its side/section' indicates the source of the flow; here برش functions as 'side/part' (poetic use).
segments.s617-g29: فرود آمد is a fixed past expression 'he alighted/descended'. و links to the next action. خنجری is the indefinite object 'a dagger' (no را). برکشید is the past of برکشیدن 'to draw/raise' (here 'to draw a dagger'), so the clause means 'he alighted and drew a dagger.'
segments.s617-g30: سراسر 'throughout/entirely' modifies the cutting action that follows. بر اژدها is the prepositional object 'upon the dragon', and بردرید is the past tense 'he tore/cut apart' (an intensive perfective), so the phrase says he 'cut the dragon completely through'.
segments.s617-g31: یکی مرد 'a certain man' introduces the person; برنا is an epithet 'young/strapping' following the noun (poetic order). فروبرده بود is a compound past: فروبرده (plunged/struck down) plus بود (past auxiliary) gives 'had been plunged/was struck down' — i.e., he lay struck down.
segments.s617-g32: به خون و به زهر uses به 'by/with' (here indicating afflicted/affected by) with خون 'blood' and زهر 'poison' to describe his condition. اندر 'in/within' pairs with افسرده 'dejected/suppressed' and بود (again) gives the past 'he was dejected inside' — overall: he was struck down, soaked in blood and poison, and inwardly dejected.
segments.s617-g201: نگه کن is an imperative compound: نگه (imperative 'look/watch') + کن ('do!'). It issues an instruction and is followed by the noun phrase that specifies what to watch: 'یکی شاخ بر در بلند' = 'a single/tall branch upon the door.' Word order is verb (imperative) + NP; بر در is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the door.'
segments.s617-g202: نباید is an impersonal modal 'should not / must not' introducing the following clause with که. The clause 'از باد یابد گزند' uses یابد in the subjunctive/present-tense sense 'receive' (here 'may receive' or 'will receive' in English). از + باد expresses the source/agent of harm: 'harm from the wind.'
segments.s617-g203: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that / thereafter.' ببین is an imperative 'watch / look' and governs the temporal subordinate clause تا که آید ز راه ('until he/she/one comes from the road'), where تا که + present-subjunctive آید marks a future/anticipated action.
segments.s617-g204: همی کن is a continuative imperative 'keep doing / continue to do.' بدین here is the prepositional/compound use 'با + این' ('with this' / 'by this'), followed by تازیانه (object 'whip') and نگاه used nominally to mean 'watch/guard' or 'keep (it) ready' — so the whole clause instructs to 'keep/hold this whip ready' or 'keep watch with this whip.'
segments.s617-g205: خداوند خانه is a nominal phrase 'the lord/master of the house' (title + noun). بپویید is an older/polite past-form verb meaning 'sought/searched / hastened about' and is modified by سخت ('strongly/urgently'), so the clause reads 'the house-master searched/hurried about the house vigorously.'
segments.s617-g206: بیاویخت is a past/archaic verb 'he hung / he let hang' and takes the following NP آن شیب شاه as its object. از درخت with آویخت/آویختن is idiomatic in Persian for 'hung from the tree' (از expresses the point of suspension). Word order here places the verb before its object, a feature common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s617-g207: همی داشت ... نگاه uses همی + داشت + نگاه to form a continuous past sense: 'he was keeping watch/was watching.' آن را is the definite object (آن = 'that', را = object marker), and زمانی 'for a while' indicates duration, so together: 'he kept watching that for a while.'
segments.s617-g208: پدید آمد is a simple past 'appeared/was revealed.' از راه is a source phrase 'from the road,' and بی‌مر (an archaic/uncertain epithet here) qualifies سپاه 'host/army' placed after it: 'from the road appeared a (bi-mar) host' — rendered naturally as 'a vast/countless host appeared from the road.'
segments.s617-g81: Word order is object (بره) + verbal stem (کشت) + modal predicate (باید) with the dative/second-person clitic (ت) attached: together they mean 'you must kill the lamb.' را here is the emphatic/definite object marker following the clitic; کاین سوار begins the relative/explanatory clause 'this rider...'.
segments.s617-g82: بزرگست is an older fused copular form meaning 'is great' (بزرگ + ­ست = 'is great'). و links the next phrase; از تخمه شهریار is a genitive/lineage phrase 'from the seed (offspring) of the king,' i.e. 'of royal descent.'
segments.s617-g83: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'); فر is the technical concept of royal/divine glory, modified by کیان ('of the Kiyans' — the royal house). دارد is the present-tense possessive 'has/possesses.' و links to the following noun phrase نور ماه ('the moon's light').
segments.s617-g84: نماند is a present/stative verb 'remain/ be left' used impersonally here; همی is an emphatic/old adverbial particle (here reinforcing exclusivity), جز means 'except,' and به marks the beneficiary/possessor 'to/with Bahramshah' (i.e. 'it remains only with Bahramshah').
segments.s617-g85: چنین modifies گفت ('thus he said'); با زن گرانمایه is the prepositional phrase 'to/with the noble/esteemed woman.' شوی is the archaic second-person present/subjunctive of شدن ('become/are') and here signals the start of his direct address to her (conventional older phrasing when speaking to 'you').
segments.s617-g86: که introduces the content of his speech ('that' / 'namely'); چندین چرا is literally 'several/many whys' — a poetic way to say 'many questions'; بایدت is the modal 'it is necessary for you' with the enclitic -ت marking 'you'; گفت‌وگوی is the noun 'conversation/questions' (object of the modal), so the clause means 'that you must have so many questions' i.e. 'why do you need to ask so many questions?'.
segments.s617-g87: نداری here expresses lack/absence ('you do not have' / 'you lack'); نمکسود (نمک‌سود) is a food item 'salted [meat/fish]' and functions as one item lacking; و links items; هیزم نه نان lists further lacks ('no firewood nor bread'), with نه negating the final item in the series.
segments.s617-g88: چه is an interrogative 'what/how'; سازی (from ساختن) here is a second-person future/optative idiom 'what will you do / how will you manage'; تو is the pronoun 'you'; برگ 'leaf' is literal but used figuratively as 'a single scrap/bit'; چنین میهمان 'such a guest' is the object — together: 'How will you manage with a leaf for such a guest?'.
segments.s617-g49: چنین is an adverb placed up front meaning 'thus' and frames the action. تا introduces the limit 'until' and combines with the directional phrase به آباد جایی ('to an inhabited place'); رسید is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular perfect 'arrived/reached' that closes the clause.
segments.s617-g50: Two locative phrases are stacked in parallel: به هامون ('to the Hamun/plain') and به نزد سرایی ('to/near a dwelling'). نزد here means 'near' or 'in the vicinity of' and سرایی is used in the sense 'a dwelling/house'. The verb رسید repeats to echo the preceding clause and is again 3rd‑person past 'arrived'.
segments.s617-g51: زنی دید is a simple past clause 'he saw a woman' (زنی = 'a woman', دید = 'he saw'). The following locative string بر کتف او بر سبوی is a compact, poetic description: بر marks position ('on'), کتف او = 'her shoulder' (او as possessive 'her'), and بر سبوی indicates the object carried there ('an ewer' on the shoulder). Word order places the possessor and carried object after the body‑part phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s617-g52: ز here introduces the source/agent or cause sense 'from/because of'. The sequence بهرام خسرو names the person (Bahram Khosrow). بپوشید is a past, 3rd‑person singular form meaning 'she covered/concealed' (note the prefixed ب‑ common in classical usage), and روی is the direct object 'her face'. The clause means she hid/covered her face on account of (or in the presence of) Bahram Khosrow.
segments.s617-g53: بدو is a dative clitic/phrase 'to him' (meaning 'unto him' or 'to that person'), and گفت functions as the reporting verb 'said' in past tense. The intervening sequence (کایدر سپنج) contains an archaic or dialectal vocative/interjection (کایدر) addressing the addressee and the noun سپنج 'sponge' as the object requested; the full sense is 'Bahram said to him: O — (give) a sponge.'
segments.s617-g54: دهید is an imperative/optative 2nd‑person plural or polite form of 'give'. ار نه is the conditional 'if not / otherwise'. باید is an impersonal 'it is necessary' and is followed by the infinitive گذشتن 'to pass' to form 'it will be necessary to pass'. به رنج 'with hardship' is an adverbial complement; together: 'Give (the sponge), otherwise one must pass with hardship.'
segments.s617-g55: چنین گفت زن = 'thus said the woman' (چنین fronting the clause). کای is a vocative particle 'O' (archaic), and نبرده is a compound negative past meaning 'has not taken' (or 'you did not take'); سوار is the noun 'rider/horse' or 'mount' here acting as the direct object of نبرده. The clause is an address to the arriving rider, noting he has no mount.
segments.s617-g56: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'; این خانه is the object 'this house'. چون introduces a simile/comparison 'as' and خانه خویش is 'your own house' formed by خوی (root 'kin/own') plus the enclitic ش marking possession. دار is an imperative/formal command here meaning 'keep/treat' (classical usage of داشتن/دار) — together: 'You, treat this house as your own.'
segments.s617-g225: بدو is the prepositional/clitic form 'to him/to you' placed before the verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so treat it as the past tense verb here). "کای روزبه" is a vocative construction: کای is used as an address particle 'O' before the name روزبه. The sequence reflects poetic/inverted word order: indirect object (بدو) + verb (گفت) + subject/name and vocative.
segments.s617-g226: ترا is the object clitic (ت + را) meaning 'to you/you (as object)' and precedes the verb; دادم is 1st person singular past 'I gave'. The direct objects follow as demonstrative noun phrases: این مرز ('this border') و این خوب ده ('and this good/beautiful village') — خوب is an adjective modifying ده (village). The clause thus literally reads 'To you I gave this border and this good village.'
segments.s617-g227: همیشه is the adverb 'always'; جز از is the exception phrase 'except for/except in'; میزبانی is the noun 'hosting/receiving guests'. مکن is the prohibitive/negative imperative 'do not' (here forbidding unspecified activities except hospitality). The clause therefore means 'Always, except for hosting, do not [do those other things].'
segments.s617-g228: برین باش is an imperative admonition: برین combines بر ('on/upon') with -ین (here the orthographic/grammatical element related to این), giving a sense 'be over/above/ supreme here', and باش is the 2nd‑person imperative 'be'. و links the clauses; پالیزبانی is 'orchard‑keeping/gardener‑duty' and مکن again negates it ('do not be an orchard‑keeper'). The pair gives two commands: 'Be the lord/overseer, and do not take the role of gardener.'
segments.s617-g229: بگفت is the past narrative 'he said'; این points back to 'this (thing just said)'; و links to the next clause. خندان is 'smiling', and بشد is the past/resultative sense 'he became/it happened (that he was smiling)' — together 'he said this and smiled'. زان = 'from/thereupon'; سرای here means 'house/room' (the provided lemma senses were for a different root, so the given lemma senses do not match the locative noun).
segments.s617-g230: نشست is the past verb 'he sat down' (not the noun 'session' here); از بر is a compound prepositional flavor ('from/on/at') that often appears in older/poetic Persian; باره here means 'occasion/instance', and بادپای is an epithet 'swift‑footed' (descriptive adjective/nickname). The phrase indicates he seated himself in/at that house on that occasion, near the swift‑footed one.
segments.s617-g231: بشد is here narrative 'it happened/then', marking a new event sequence; زان ('from that') combines with ده (village) in the running text to give 'from that village' (ده is present in the surface text though not listed among the provided words). بی‌نوا is an adjectival epithet 'pitiable/hapless' modifying شهریار 'ruler/king'.
segments.s617-g232: بیامد is the past verb 'he came'; به is the preposition 'to/into'; ایوان is the place‑noun 'iwan/portico' and گوهرنگار is an adjective/epithet 'jewel‑adorned' modifying it. The clause states that the pitiable ruler came into the jewel‑decorated porch.
segments.s617-g113: زن کم‌سخن is a noun phrase with the noun (زن) followed by the descriptive compound adjective (کم‌سخن 'few‑spoken / taciturn'). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' placed after the subject (typical V‑final narrative order). آری is an interjection 'indeed/yes', and نکوست is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'is good' (نکو + است) serving as the predicate of the quoted speech.
segments.s617-g114: هم at the start is an adverb 'also/indeed' linking what follows to the prior clause. آغاز هر کار is a noun phrase 'the beginning of every work' (noun + quantifier phrase), و connects the parallel noun فرجام 'the end/outcome'. ازوست is a poetic contraction of از او است 'is from her/him/it' (copula fused as -ست); it predicates that both the beginning and the end originate 'from her'.
segments.s617-g115: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the past tense 'said' (verb), with the speaker's name بهرام placed after the verb (a somewhat inverted order: indirect object — verb — subject). کاین است و بس is a short clause 'this is (so) and enough' (کاین 'this/that', است copula, بس 'enough/that suffices').
segments.s617-g116: ازو is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from him/of him' marking the source of the following nominal idea. دادجویی is a noun meaning 'seeking justice / a claim (bringing suit)'; نبینند is a negative-subjunctive/optative third‑person plural form 'let them not see / there be no' used here to express prohibition or a wish. کس functions as an indefinite 'anyone/someone' in the scope of the negation—i.e. 'that no one should seek justice from him.'
segments.s617-g117: زن برمنش is a compound: زن 'the woman' followed by an epithet برمنش 'of good/noble disposition'. گفت is the past 'said' (verb). کای is an archaic vocative particle 'O' used to address someone directly (common in epic diction). پاک‌رای is the vocative epithet 'pure‑minded / of pure counsel' addressing the addressee.
segments.s617-g118: برین is written as two pieces here: بر (a preposition 'on/at/in') + ین (orthographic variant of این 'this'), so برین ده = 'in/at this village' (ده = village). فراوان is an adverb/adjectival 'many/abundant' and کس 'people/persons' is the noun being quantified; است is the copula 'is/are' completing the existential statement. و سرای coordinates another nominal 'and dwellings/inns/houses' (سرای) — note the poet omits ezāfe linking and uses simple coordination.
segments.s617-g119: همیشه is a fronted adverb 'always'. گذار سواران is a nominal compound 'the passage/coming of the horsemen' (the possessor group 'سواران' follows the head noun without an explicit ezāfe in poetic style). بود is the past copula/existential 'there was' indicating existence in the past.
segments.s617-g120: ز is a poetic preposition 'from'. دیوان here means 'the court/administrative bureau' (a common epic/administrative sense). The phrase و از repeats the source-marking (و = 'and', از = 'from') before کارداران 'the officials/managers', and بود closes the clause with the past existential 'there was'. The repeated prepositions and simple coordination are stylistic choices typical of epic diction.
segments.s617-g57: چو is a subordinating particle meaning “when” introducing a temporal clause. پاسخ شنید is a past-tense clause (شنید, ‘he heard’) with پاسخ as its object: “he heard the answer.” اسپ در خانه راند is a past clause where راند (‘drove’) is the main verb; اسپ is the (omitted/understood) object ‘steed/horse’ and در خانه indicates the goal ‘into the house.’
segments.s617-g58: زن میزبان is a noun‑noun combination = “the hostess/the host’s wife” (the woman acting as host). شوی here functions as a participial descriptor (literally “having become”) attached to زن, so the phrase reads like “the woman who had become host.” را is the definite object-marker (marking the person called), and پیش خواند is the past verb phrase “called/summoned forward.”
segments.s617-g59: بدو is a prepositional/clitic phrase meaning “to him” (direction of speech). گفت here is the past verb “said” introducing direct speech. کاه آر is an imperative/command: کاه “straw” plus آر (imperative ‘bring’/‘fetch’ in classical usage) = “bring straw.” و links the commands. اسپش بمال: اسپش = اسپ + enclitic possessive ش ‘his steed’, and بمال is an imperative ‘rub!’ so “rub his horse.”
segments.s617-g60: چو marks a temporal subordinate clause “when.” گاه جو آید = “when the time for barley comes” (گاه = time/occasion, جو = barley, آید = comes). بکن is an imperative ‘put/do’ here meaning ‘put (it).’ در جوال = ‘into the sack’ (در = into/in, جوال = sack). The clause gives a conditional/temporal instruction tied to arrival of barley.
segments.s617-g61: خود آمد uses خود as an emphatic/reflexive adverb “himself” + آمد (past “came”), i.e. “he himself came.” به جایی que clause: به جایی که بودش نهفت = “to the place where it was (بودش) hidden (نهفت).” بودش is the past form with enclitic ش ‘it’: “where it was,” and نهفت is a past/predicative adjective/verb meaning “concealed/hidden.”
segments.s617-g62: ز پیش indicates a temporal/locative relation “from before” or “previously.” اندرون رفت is a past clause: اندرون = inside/inner quarters, رفت = “went” or “entered.” و links clauses. خانه برفت: خانه is ‘the house,’ and برفت is a past verb (here meaning ‘went into/entered the house’); the sequence narrates movement: from before he went inwards and entered the house.
segments.s617-g63: حصیری بگسترد is a past action: حصیری (a woven straw mat) + بگسترد (past “spread”/“laid out”) = “he spread a mat.” و links the next action. بالش نهاد uses بالش (pillow) as object and نهاد (past of نهادن, “placed”) = “he put/placed a pillow.” The verbs are simple past narrative forms describing successive actions.
segments.s617-g64: به بهرام is a prepositional phrase “toward/for Bahram” (به + proper name). بر آفرین کرد یاد is an idiomatic clause: بر آفرین کرد = “offered praise” (آفرین here as acclamation/praise, کرد = “made/did”), and یاد means “remembrance” or “he recalled/remembered.” Altogether: “he remembered Bahram with praise” or “he praised/recalled Bahram.”
segments.s617-g65: سوی خانه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward the house' (preposition before noun). آب شد is a terse, poetic way to report an event with شد used like an intransitive past (here 'water came/flowed'); the repeated آب followed by برد is a compact clause meaning 'the water carried (it) away' — برد is here the past verb 'took/carried' used after the noun it refers to. The overall word order is topical/prepositional fronting (سوی خانه) then the event.
segments.s617-g66: همی is an adverbial particle giving immediacy ('then/just then'); در نهان is a locative phrase 'in secret' (در + adverb/adj نهان). شوی here is an inherited/poetic form referring to a 'washing' or 'bath' (object) and is marked by را; برشمرد is a past-tense verb 'he counted/enumerated.' Grammatically, the phrase places the adverb (همی) before the locative (در نهان) and then the object (شوی را) before the finite verb برشمرد.
segments.s617-g67: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that.' این پیر و ابله is the noun phrase 'this old and foolish one.' بماند is the present/subjunctive (or jussive) third-person form of ماندن ('let him remain / should remain'), giving a hortative/reporting sense; به جای is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in place / in his place.'
segments.s617-g68: هرانگه که is a temporal conjunction 'whenever/whensoever that'; بیند is the present/subjunctive/indefinite form of دیدن 'sees' used in generalizing temporal clauses; کس functions as an indefinite subject 'anyone/someone'; اندر سرای is a locative phrase 'inside the house/chamber.' Word order here is conjunction + verb + indefinite subject + locative.
segments.s617-g69: نباشد is an impersonal negative verb form meaning 'it is not (the case)' or 'there ought not to be'; چنین کار کار زنان is a doubled, emphatic noun phrase literally 'such work, the work of women' (the repetition کار is emphatic). Together the clause denies that the described action belongs to women: 'Such work is not women's work.'
segments.s617-g70: منم is emphatic 'I am' (subject + enclitic emphatic suffix). لشکری‌دار is a predicate noun 'commander/one who has troops.' دندان کنان is a participial/adverbial construction: دندان 'tooth' with the suffixive participle کنان ('doing/doing so') yields 'baring/gnashing the teeth' — a manner adverbial modifying the verbal or nominal predicate.
segments.s617-g71: بشد is a compact narrational verb (here 'then happened/so he') introducing the action; شاه بهرام is the subject ('King Bahram'). و links the clauses and رخ را بشست places رخ ('face') as the direct object marked by را and بشست is the past tense 'he washed.' Word order: narrative verb + subject, then coordinated clause with object before verb.
segments.s617-g72: کزان is a causal/relative particle 'from which / because of which' (here giving the reason). اژدها بود is a simple past copula 'the dragon was'; ناتن is the proper name, and درست here functions as an intensifier 'indeed/truly/just so.' The clause therefore gives the reason: 'for that dragon was indeed Natan.'
segments.s617-g185: 'تو' is the 2nd‑singular subject 'you'; 'بیداد را' is the definite/direct object (را marks it as the object); 'کرده‌ای' is the perfect/past‑result form 'you have done' (2nd sing.), formed from کرده + ای; the noun 'دادگر' follows the verb and here functions as an address/predicative noun ('O just one' or ironically 'just one') commenting on the person who performed the action.
segments.s617-g186: 'وگرنه' introduces a contrary‑to‑fact/otherwise clause; 'نبودی' is the past (counterfactual) form 'you would not be' (negative past/conditional sense of بودن); 'ورا' here is an indirect/benefactive marker 'for him/to him/for that'; 'این هنر' = 'this skill/ability' (the thing being ascribed). Together: 'otherwise he would not have this skill' (literally: 'you would not be, for this skill').
segments.s617-g187: 'ازان پس' is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'thereupon/after that'; 'چنین گفت' is a formulaic 'thus said' (چنین modifies گفت); 'با کدخدای' is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the headman' indicating the addressee/companion of the speech.
segments.s617-g188: 'که' introduces reported content ('that'); 'بیداد را' is the object 'injustice' (را marks it definite); 'داد' functions here as the noun 'justice' and with 'شد' the sequence expresses a passive/result 'justice was done/occurred'; 'باز' is the adverb 'again'; 'جای' 'place' (here idiomatically meaning the proper order/position). The clause thus reports that justice was restored/that wrongdoing was again made right.
segments.s617-g189: 'تو' = 'you' (address); 'با خنده و رامشی' is a manner phrase 'with a smile and with calmness/tranquillity' (با + nouns linked by و); 'باش' is the 2nd‑singular imperative 'be!'; 'زین' is a classical particle 'for this / because of this' (shortened from از این) introducing the reason clause that follows.
segments.s617-g190: 'که' introduces the explanatory clause 'for/because'; 'بخشود' is 3rd‑singular past 'forgave' or 'has pardoned'; 'بر ما' marks the indirect object 'toward/over us' (here 'forgave us'); 'جهان‌آفرین' is the agent/nominative 'the World‑Creator' placed after the verb (poetic inversion common in classical Persian).
segments.s617-g191: 'به هرکاره' is an adverbial 'in/for every task'; 'چون' here means 'when/as' or 'like' introducing the circumstance; 'شیربا' is a compound (literally 'with milk' / milk‑related) describing the manner or means; 'پخته' is a past participle 'cooked / ripened / seasoned'; 'شد' is the auxiliary/past 'became' — together: 'when (one) became seasoned/cooked as with milk' (a poetic way to say 'when one became experienced/seasoned').
segments.s617-g192: 'زن و مرد' 'women and men' (collective subjects); 'زان کار' = 'from/through that work' (زان = از آن = from that); 'پردخته' is a participle/adjective 'well‑worked, well‑seasoned, skilled'; 'شد' again is the auxiliary 'became' — 'women and men thereby became well‑trained/seasoned by that work.'
segments.s617-g33: بران is a temporal/adverbial particle meaning 'thereupon' and links this clause to what preceded. مرد is the noun 'man' (subject). بسیار modifies the verb and here means 'greatly/much'. بگریست is a past (perfective) 3rd-person singular verb 'he wept.' زار is used as a manner adverb following the verb ('lamentingly/bitterly'); in classical Persian poets often place such manner words after the verb.
segments.s617-g34: وزان is a compound adverb/conjunction 'and from that' introducing the result. زهر is used figuratively as a causal noun 'poison/bitterness' (the cause of the effect). شد is the past form of 'to become' (3rd sg). چشم here is the possessed noun 'eye(s)' (the possessor's ezāfe is omitted in poetic style: چشمِ بهرام → چشم بهرام). بهرام is the possessor/name. تار functions as a predicative adjective 'dim/blurred/wet' (in poetry adjectives often follow the noun); the structure is literally 'the eye of Bahram became تار.'
segments.s617-g35: وزانجا is a fused adverb 'and/from there' pointing to the place just mentioned. بیامد is past tense 3rd-person singular 'he came.' به is the preposition 'to/towards,' and پرده‌سرای is a compound noun referring to the curtained chamber/scene (a place reached by the verb); in poetry such locative phrases commonly follow the verb.
segments.s617-g36: می‌آورد is an imperfective-form (prefix می-) used here in narrative sense to mean 'he brought (in)' or 'would bring'—a habitual/continuous past nuance common in poetic narrative. و simply connects clauses. خوبان is the plural noun 'the beauties/beautiful ones' (here performers). بربط is adverbial/nominal indicating the instrument 'with the barbat (lute).' سرای is a verbal form meaning 'they sang/composed' (poetic infinitive/3rd-person sense), so the sequence means 'the beauties sang to the barbat.'
segments.s617-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' سی روز is a duration expression 'thirty days' (direct object of the temporal clause). بگذشت is past-tense 'passed' (3rd-person singular). ز introduces the source/time 'from/of' and precedes اردیبهشت (the month name). The clause reads 'when thirty days passed from Ordibehesht.'
segments.s617-g38: شد is the past 'became.' از introduces cause or source 'from' and governs میوه 'fruit.' پالیزها is the plural 'orchards/fields' and is the noun governed by the preceding phrase (i.e., 'the orchards, from fruit, became...'). چون is the simile 'like/as,' and بهشت is the comparison 'paradise.' Word order here places the verb 'became' before the source phrase and simile, a flexible poetic ordering.
segments.s617-g39: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so.' ساخت is past 'made' or 'devised.' کاید is a noun meaning 'plot/stratagem' and is the direct object of ساخت. به introduces the location/target 'into/to,' and تور here functions as 'net' (a literal or metaphorical place into which the plot was set). اندرون is the locative noun 'inside/inner quarters.' The whole phrase describes creating a stratagem placed 'within the inner net/space.'
segments.s617-g40: پرستنده is a noun 'worshipper/adorer' used here as 'admirer' or devoted follower. با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him.' یکی is the indefinite/number word 'one' (here 'a single' or 'one certain'), and رهنمون is the noun 'guide.' The clause is a nominal (copula 'to be' is omitted, as common in Persian poetry), meaning 'an admirer was with him as his one guide.'
segments.s617-g193: به نزدیک is a prepositional/locative phrase 'toward/near'; شد is the past/copular verb (literary past ‘came / became’), so the clause reads 'came near'. آن پاک‌رای is the postposed subject with demonstrative + epithet ('that virtuous man/steward'). Poetic word order places the locative phrase and verb before the subject.
segments.s617-g194: همی is a durative/adverbial particle (here marking ongoing action: 'was / kept on'), برد is the past form of بردن 'to carry / take' (classical usage) though that exact sense wasn't listed in the provided senses, خوان is the direct object 'banquet/table', از پسش is a locative phrase 'after/behind him', and کدخدای is the agent (headman) placed after the verb in this poetic inversion.
segments.s617-g195: نهاده is a perfective/past participial form 'placed/put (down)'; بدو is the indirect-object clitic-like phrase 'to him/unto him'; کاسه شیربا is the direct object 'a bowl of milk'. Word order here is verb – indirect object – direct object (typical in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s617-g196: چه نیکو is an exclamatory phrase 'how good / how well'; بدی‌گر is an agent-noun 'one who does bad / evildoer'; بدی is the noun 'evil / harm' and appears here as part of the comment about what the evildoer did; زیربا is an obscure/lexical item (probably a specific object/name) whose precise meaning is uncertain in the lexicon provided, so the line reads like an ironical or critical exclamation about the evildoer giving that thing.
segments.s617-g197: ازان شیربا is a prepositional phrase 'from that milk'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; لختی is an adverbial 'for a short while' or 'a little (bit)'; بخورد is the classical past/present form of خوردن with the prefix ب- indicating the (perfective/habitual) action here 'he ate/drank'. The clause means 'the king drank a little of that milk.'
segments.s617-g198: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = past 'said'); پس is temporal 'then'; با governs the following noun phrase meaning 'to/with'; زن رادمرد is 'the wife of the nobleman' (noun + genitival epithet). Note: the lemma senses provided for گفت were dialogue-related nouns, so I mark its sense as unmatched to reflect that it's here the past verb 'said.'
segments.s617-g199: که introduces a subordinate/quotative clause 'that / (saying) that'; این تازیانه is the demonstrative + object 'this whip'; به درگاه is the locative 'on/at the threshold' and بر here is the prepositional particle 'on/upon' that pairs with the following verb (بر بیاویز = 'hang upon').
segments.s617-g200: بیاویز is the imperative 'hang (it)'; جایی is 'a place / somewhere'; که introduces a relative clause 'where / that'; باشد is the subjunctive/present-form 'there be' (used here in a relative clause: 'where there is'); گذر is 'passage / thoroughfare'. Together: 'hang (it) somewhere where there is a passage.'
segments.s617-g89: A sequence of coordinated past actions: two verbs (خورد, رفت) are linked with و and the agent phrase این سوار comes after them (object/verb before explicit subject is common in poetry). کشتی here appears in the place of a past action relating to the lamb (archaic/poetic spelling or form); خورد is the past of “to eat”; رفت is the past meaning “left/rode off.”
segments.s617-g90: An imperative address to the addressee: تو شو is the vocative + imperative “you, become”; خر is the predicative noun (‘a donkey’). The following phrase (به انبوهی اندر گذار) is directional: به + place (انبوهی, ‘the crowd’) with archaic اندر ‘in/among’ and گذار used as a verb meaning ‘pass/put (yourself) into’ — i.e. ‘go into the crowd.’
segments.s617-g91: A piled list of hostile elements joined by و (and): زمستان, سرما, باد. دمان is attached to باد as a genitive/specifying noun (here read as the foothill/hem in poetic usage), so باد دمان = ‘the winds of the d(oman)/foothills.’
segments.s617-g92: به پیش آیدت is a light-clause meaning ‘will come before you’ (به + پیش + آید + ـت enclitic ‘to you’). یک زمان is an adverbial phrase ‘at one time/at once,’ and بی‌گمان is an adverb ‘undoubtedly’ placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s617-g93: همی before a verb signals iterative or continuous action (‘kept on ...’). گفت here functions as the verb ‘said/was saying’ (the provided lemma list lacks the speaking-verb sense, so that sense is not listed). نشنید is the past negative of شنیدن (‘did not hear’), and the clause is coordinated with و.
segments.s617-g94: A relative clause introduced by که (‘who/that’) describing the woman: هم ... هم is the correlative ‘both ... and ...’. نیک‌پی بود is a simple past copular clause (‘was well-born/virtuous’), and رای‌زن is a predicate noun (‘a counsellor’ or ‘one who gives counsel’).
segments.s617-g95: A passive formation: بره (subject) + کشته شد (‘was killed’). هم is emphatic ‘also/in fact,’ and به فرجام کار is a prepositional phrase ‘in/at the outcome of the matter’ (به marking the relation and فرجام کار = ‘the affair’s end/outcome’).
segments.s617-g96: به گفتار آن زن = ‘according to the speech/words of that woman’ (به ... indicating ‘by/according to’). ز بهر is a poetic prepositional sequence meaning ‘for the sake of/on account of’ and introduces the beneficiary/goal (سوار, ‘the rider’).
segments.s617-g153: ستمکاره شد شهریار جهان is built with the predicative phrase first: ستمکاره (an agent‑noun, “oppressor/tyrant”) + شد (past of شدن, “became”). شهریار جهان is the subject phrase “the king/sovereign of the world” placed after the predicate, a common poetic word order in classical Persian. Here شد is a lexical past verb meaning “became,” not an auxiliary.
segments.s617-g154: دلش دوش پیچان شد اندر نهان: دلش is دل + enclitic ش (his), so “his heart.” دوش is an adverb meaning “last night.” پیچان is a participial/adjectival form meaning “twisting/agonized”; پیچان شد = “became twisted/was in turmoil.” اندر نهان is a locative phrase (“in/within hiddenness,” i.e. “secretly”). Note the order: time (دوش) follows the possessor phrase before the verb complex.
segments.s617-g155: بدو گفت introduces direct speech: بدو = “to him” (dative) and گفت is the past tense ‘said.’ What follows is the reported question. شوی here is an archaic 2nd‑person/colloquial particle of address (roughly ‘you’/‘O you’) before the question; از چه گویی همی = “from what / why do you say?” where گویی is 2nd‑person present ‘you say’ and همی is the old present‑continuous/affirmative particle.
segments.s617-g156: به فال بد اندر چه جویی همی: به فال بد = “toward / about an ill omen” (a fixed phrase meaning ‘with regard to a bad omen’). اندر چه جویی همی is an interrogative clause: اندر = poetic ‘in/into,’ چه = ‘what/why,’ جویی is 2nd‑person present of جستن (to seek/search) ‘you seek,’ and همی marks the continuous/present. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: literal ‘into what do you seek’ = ‘why do you seek (it).’
segments.s617-g157: چنین گفت زن = “thus said the woman”: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so,’ گفت is the past ‘said,’ and زن is the subject. کای is a vocative particle ‘O,’ followed by the epithet گرانمایه “precious/esteemed.” شوی here functions as the addressed second‑person form (an archaic/poetic address ‘you’ or ‘you are’), completing the vocative phrase.
segments.s617-g158: مرا بیهده نیست این گفت‌وگوی is a topical/inverted order: مرا = “to me” (object/experiencer), بیهده نیست = “is not idle/futile” (بیهده adjective + negated copula نیست), and این گفت‌وگوی = “this conversation/talk” (the grammatical subject placed at the end). Literal: “To me, not idle is this conversation.”
segments.s617-g159: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” بیدادگر is a predicative noun/adjective “oppressive/tyrannical,” and شد is the past ‘became’; جهاندار شاه is the subject phrase “the world‑holding king/sovereign” (appositive). The clause therefore reads “When the sovereign became tyrannical….”
segments.s617-g160: ز گردون = poetic prepositional phrase ‘from the sky/heaven’ (گردون = heaven/fate). نتابد is a negative present/past‑relative of تابیدن; here it carries the sense “does not/ would not endure” ( تابیدن can mean ‘to endure’ or ‘to bear/shine’ depending on context). ببایست is an impersonal/modal form meaning “it is necessary / must,” and ماه is the noun “moon” (the thing that consequently must fall/ be affected). The clause expresses that heaven will not put up with the tyrant and the moon must therefore be brought low.
segments.s617-g217: که شاها is a vocative/exclamatory address: 'که' here introduces direct address and 'شاها' is the vocative 'O king!'. بزرگا ردا بخردا is an older word order where ردا (robe) is named and بخردا (به + خردا) marks the recipient 'to the wise'; بزرگا 'the great (ones)' is another recipient in the same series. Note the omission of an explicit verb 'give' in this clause — the giving/command sense is supplied by the wider context.
segments.s617-g218: جهاندار is an appositive/title 'the sovereign' added to the list. و بر موبدان موبدا uses بر + plural موبدان 'over/above the mobeds' to indicate superiority, and موبدا is the singular/archaic form 'mobed' (the chief priest) in apposition. Together this gives 'and the sovereign, and the chief over the mobeds.'
segments.s617-g219: بدین خانه is the locative 'in/at this house' formed from ب (به 'to/at') + a following element; خانه is the place. درویش functions as the topical subject 'the dervish' and بد modifies میزبان producing 'a bad/ill host.' The clause thus says that, at that house, the dervish had a bad host.
segments.s617-g220: زنی بی‌نوا is a noun phrase 'a poor/pitiable woman.' شوی here functions as an archaic linking/copular element (contextually 'was' / 'served as' or indicates relation to the following title) and پالیزبان names the occupation 'gardener/caretaker'. Together the phrase identifies a poor woman in relation to the gardener (i.e. she was the gardener’s wife / she belonged to the gardener’s household), with شوی as the older linking form.
segments.s617-g221: بران here = 'on that' / 'for that', introducing the topic. بندگی is the noun 'servitude/devotion' and is the object/reason for the following action. نیز 'also' adds the sense of an additional act, and پوزش نمود is a verbalized noun + نمودن ('to show, do') meaning 'made an apology' or 'begged pardon'.
segments.s617-g222: همان شاه 'that same king' is the subject. ما را uses the object marker را after the pronoun 'us'. پژوهش نمود is the noun 'inquiry/investigation' used with نمودن to mean 'made inquiry' or 'questioned/examined.' Word order is subject — object (marked) — verbal noun + نمود.
segments.s617-g223: که چون … رسید is a temporal clause 'when/when as … arrived.' چون here functions in the temporal sense 'when/as', تو is the addressed 'you', and بدین جای مهمان is 'to this place as a guest' (بدین = به + این 'to this', جای = place, مهمان = guest). رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached.'
segments.s617-g224: بدین بی‌نوا خانه و مان reached repeats/continues the arrival location: 'to this poor house and dwelling.' بدین (به + این) marks 'to this', بی‌نوا modifies خانه 'poor house', و links خانه and مان, and رسید closes with 'arrived/reached.' مان here names a secondary noun 'dwelling/abode' (a paired term with خانه).
segments.s617-g121: یکی is an indefinite subject meaning "someone." نام دزدی is a noun phrase (= "the name 'thief'" or 'the label of theft') functioning as the object being placed; نهد is the 3rd-person-singular present/presentive form of نهاد "to put/place," and بر کسی is a prepositional phrase "on/to someone" marking who receives the label. Word order is Subject (یکی) + Object (نام دزدی) + Verb (نهد) + prepositional complement (بر کسی).
segments.s617-g122: که introduces a relative clause "who/that." فرجام here means "end/outcome" and زان is the enclitic/poetic contraction of از آن "from that," referring back to the mentioned injury; رنج is "suffering" and یابد (3rd-sg. of یافتن) means "finds/comes to experience." بسی is an adverb meaning "much/abundantly." The clause literally: "who in the end finds much suffering from that."
segments.s617-g123: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning "for the sake of." درم is the small coin (dirham) that motivates the action. گرددش is گردد (3rd-sg. of گردیدن "to turn/become") with the clitic ـش "him/his," so the structure is "he becomes (for himself) ..." and کینه‌کش is a predicate noun "a vindictive person/avenger." The line means "for a dirham he turns into a vindictive man."
