segments.s617-g124: که introduces a defining relative clause "who." ناخوش is an adjective "unhappy/displeased," and کند is 3rd-sg. of کردن used as a causative "makes." بر دلش = "upon his heart" (دل + possessive clitic ش = "his heart"). روز خوش "pleasant/happy day(s)" is the object affected. The whole clause: "who makes the happy days of his heart (i.e. his happy days) unhappy."
segments.s617-g125: زن پاک‌تن is a noun phrase "a pure/virtuous woman" (پاک‌تن = "pure-bodied/virtuous"). را is the definite-object marker marking this woman as the direct object. به آلودگی is a prepositional-result phrase "into defilement/impurity" (آلودگی = moral/physical pollution). The phrase shows result: he causes the virtuous woman to fall into impurity.
segments.s617-g126: برد here functions as a verbal idea "takes/puts/causes" (classical usage; context = 'takes/ruins'); نام is "name/reputation" (here reputation). و = "and." آرد is 3rd-sg. present of آوردن "to bring" (so "and brings"), and به بیهودگی = "to futility/meaninglessness." Together: "he ruins her reputation and brings her name to futility."
segments.s617-g127: زیانی بود is a nominal predication "it was a loss/harm." کان is an archaic connective (classical relative/that) introducing the following clause. نیابد is the negative present/ability sense of یافتن "is not found/one cannot obtain," so کان نیابد به گنج means "which cannot be made up/obtained by treasure." به گنج = "with/by treasure."
segments.s617-g128: ز is the poetic preposition "from/because of." شاه جهاندار is a title phrase "the king, the world-holder/sovereign." اینست is the copula "this is," and رنج is "suffering/hardship." Together the clause reads "This suffering comes from the king, the sovereign" (i.e. the king is the source of this hardship).
segments.s617-g105: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (classical use). شب is the noun 'night'. کرد is the past of کردن used here in a causative/becoming sense—classical/poetic usage meaning 'it became' or 'fell' (i.e. 'night fell'). با introduces accompaniment ('with' / 'to' the company of) and is followed by a poetic noun phrase آفتاب انجمن ('sun' + 'assembly'), literally 'the sun's assembly' or 'the assembly'; word order is typical of older Persian poetry where adverbial/prepositional phrases may intervene before the following clause.
segments.s617-g106: This is a simple nominal list (کدوی، می‌و، سنجد) followed by the verb آورد 'brought' and the agent زن 'the woman' placed after the verb (a common poetic order: objects before verb; subject can follow). می‌و is an archaic/poetic spelling for fruit (میوه). The list is direct objects of آورد.
segments.s617-g107: بدو is the dative/enclitic form 'to her' (to that person). گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' (classical prose/poetic word-order allows verb before subject). شاه is the subject 'the king' (positioned after the verb); the attached ای (written here joined as شاه‌ای) is the vocative/particle marking the direct address that follows. زن کم‌سخن is the vocative 'O woman of few words'—کم‌سخن is an adjective placed after the noun.
segments.s617-g108: یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one' (here introducing an indefinite tale). داستان is 'story/tale', and گوی is the classical verbal form meaning 'speak!' or 'tell!' (an imperative/command in older Persian: داستان گوی = 'tell a tale'). با من is a prepositional phrase 'to me' (literally 'with me' but idiomatically 'for me'/'to me'). کهن is a postpositive adjective 'old/ancient' modifying داستان. Overall order is imperative: 'Tell me one old tale.'
segments.s617-g109: بدان is the fused imperative 'know this' (ب prefix + دان 'know'); تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that' or 'in order that'. به گفتار تو literally 'by/at your speech' (means 'at your telling' or 'through your words'). می‌خوریم is the present plural from خوردن 'we eat/drink'—in older/poetic Persian خوردن commonly covers 'drink' as well, so here it means 'we shall partake (drink / be entertained)'.
segments.s617-g110: به is the preposition 'with/by'. می‌درد is a present-tense verb (from دریدن) 'it tears/rends' used metaphorically here—poetic language often pairs wine and 'tearing' of sorrow. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. اندوه را marks 'sorrow' as the definite object (را). بشکریم is a first-person-plural verbal form; in older usage it can mean 'we shall break/overcome' or 'we shall give thanks' (the underlying root شکر/شکستن is ambiguous in manuscripts), so contextually it means 'we will dispel (or be rid of) sorrow / we will give thanks'—the form is present/subjunctive in tone.
segments.s617-g111: بتو is the dative 'to you'. داستان is the noun 'tale/story' and نیز is the adverb 'also/too'. کردم is the past first-person 'I did / I told' (کردم = 'I have told'). یله is an adjective/adverb meaning 'loose, unrestrained, freely'—here it modifies how the tale was told ('I told freely'). Word order places the object (داستان) before the verb, as in poetic prose.
segments.s617-g112: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (equivalent to modern از). بهرامت is the name بهرام with a final -ت (here a pronominal/poetic ending—contextually 'from Bahram' or 'by Bahram'); آزادیست is the fused predicate 'it is freedom' (آزادی + ست/است = 'is liberation/relief'). ار is an archaic conditional 'if'. گله is the noun 'complaint'—so the clause reads literally 'from Bahram is deliverance, if (there be) complaint.'
